title: Wooden Egg Holder
summary: A pair of drilled shelves holding a dozen eggs on display, with a taller narrow version that doubles the capacity.
description:
Two through-holed shelves and two sides is the whole build — the only technique that matters is drilling clean 1-1/2" holes without blowing out the back face of the board. A hole saw is the cleanest tool for the job; with a spade bit, drill halfway from one face, flip the board, and finish from the other side rather than pushing straight through, which is what tears out the far side.

This plan covers two sizes: a wider 1x6 version holding a dozen eggs, and a taller, narrower 1x4 version that holds two dozen. The narrower version is noticeably less stable on its own — the source recommends anchoring it to a wall if you go that route rather than leaving it freestanding on a counter.

tools.note: 1-1/2" — the cleanest way to bore the egg holes. A spade or forstner bit works as a substitute.

materials.name: Pine, 1x6, 5 ft
materials.note: For the double-wide version, holds one dozen eggs.

cutList.note: Drill 1-1/2" holes at the marked center points before assembly.

steps.title: Drill the egg holes
steps.body: Mark the center points for each hole on the two shelf boards, then drill. Make a few practice holes in scrap first to check your technique, and clamp the board down securely before drilling. A hole saw is the cleanest method. With a spade bit, drill halfway through from the top face, flip the board, and finish from the back — pushing straight through from one side tears out the far face. Lightly sand the hole edges smooth once you're done.

steps.title: Assemble the holder
steps.body: Attach the sides to the egg shelves, two screws per joint. Brad nails and wood glue, or 1-1/4" pocket holes set to 3/4", both work as alternatives. If using pocket holes, attach the top shelf first from underneath, then the bottom shelf from underneath, so you can still fit a drill inside as the piece closes up.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand and finish. For the taller, narrower two-dozen-egg version built from 1x4s instead, follow the same steps — just note it's less stable freestanding, so consider anchoring it to a wall.

images.alt: A wooden countertop egg holder with two drilled shelves displaying a dozen eggs
